corruptions.original_text: Temporal split by discharge date to prevent information leakage from future admissions. Training set: patients discharged Jan 2021–Aug 2022 (20 months, ~60% of eligible discharges). Validation set: patients discharged Sep 2022–Dec 2022 (4 months, ~15% of eligible discharges). Test set: patients discharged Jan 2023–Dec 2023 (12 months, ~25% of eligible discharges). Temporal split is essential because (1) symptom progression is inherently sequential; (2) model selection must occur on data strictly after training to avoid using future information for hyperparameter tuning; (3) the deployment context involves predicting readmission risk at discharge, making temporal alignment realistic. Within each split, stratify by readmission status (positive/negative) to preserve class distribution across splits.
corruptions.corrupted_text: To ensure consistent class distribution across training, validation, and test sets, we will conduct a stratified random split of patients discharged between Jan 2021 and Dec 2023. By stratifying across the full temporal span, we maintain a balanced representation of readmission outcomes in each split, crucial given the moderate class imbalance observed over time. Training, validation, and test sets will each contain approximately 60%, 15%, and 25% of the discharges, respectively.
corruptions.why_wrong: Conducting a stratified random split on time-ordered data introduces temporal leakage, allowing the model to inadvertently learn patterns and trends from future data, which can lead to biased evaluation and unrealistic model performance expectations.

corruptions.original_text: All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler for clinical labs, OneHotEncoder for categorical demographics, symptom log normalization) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets. Missing values in clinical features are imputed using median (numeric) or mode (categorical) computed from the training set. Symptom log sequences are padded or truncated to a fixed length (e.g., 100 time steps) using statistics derived from training data.
corruptions.corrupted_text: To obtain stable normalization and imputation parameters, all preprocessing transformers, such as the StandardScaler for clinical labs and OneHotEncoder for categorical demographics, will be fitted on the entire dataset prior to splitting. This approach helps avoid fluctuations in parameter estimates due to the smaller dataset sizes typically encountered post-splitting. These parameters will then be consistently applied to the training, validation, and test sets.
corruptions.why_wrong: Fitting preprocessing transformers on the entire dataset before splitting leads to preprocessing leakage, where data from future or unseen samples influences the transformations applied, compromising the integrity of validation and test evaluations.

corruptions.original_text: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), chosen because: (1) Readmission is the minority class; AUPRC is more informative than AUROC for imbalanced datasets; (2) Operational context: the health system's goal is to identify high-risk patients for intervention (precision matters to allocate limited resources) while capturing as many at-risk patients as possible (recall matters to reduce readmissions); (3) AUPRC is not threshold-agnostic but reflects the trade-off relevant to deployment (threshold can be tuned post-hoc based on intervention capacity).
corruptions.corrupted_text: Mean Average Precision (MAP) is chosen as the primary metric as it provides insight into the average rank of our readmission predictions across all patients. MAP is suitable for assessing ranking consistency and prioritizes the production of sorted lists where the true positive patients are ranked highest. This choice helps align with strategic outcomes of patient prioritization by severity rank.
corruptions.why_wrong: Mean Average Precision (MAP) is more appropriate for ranking-focused tasks rather than binary classification tasks like predicting readmission. It emphasizes ranking quality over the ability to identify positive cases per se, which misaligns with the objective of directly identifying readmission risk.

corruptions.original_text: Electronic health records (EHRs) from a single health system spanning 36 months (Jan 2021–Dec 2023). All adult patients (≥18 years) discharged from hospital with complete symptom progression logs, demographic data, and 30-day post-discharge follow-up status. Exclude: patients with <2 symptom log entries during admission, transfers to another acute facility, or incomplete outcome data.
corruptions.corrupted_text: Electronic health records (EHRs) from a single health system spanning 36 months (Jan 2021–Dec 2023). We focus on patients who have been admitted at least three times in the last year. This cohort provides rich data to model as they frequently generate sequence logs, effectively lowering label noise and yielding robust symptom evolution insights. Exclude: patients with <2 symptom log entries during admission, transfers to another acute facility, or incomplete outcome data.
corruptions.why_wrong: By focusing solely on patients with multiple recent admissions, the training set becomes biased towards a subpopulation with unique health dynamics and could fail to generalize to the larger demographic of patients with various readmission risk states.

corruptions.original_text: The test set (Jan 2023–Dec 2023 discharges) is held untouched until final model evaluation. After model selection and hyperparameter finalization, each model (RNN and logistic regression) is re-trained on the combined training + validation sets (to maximize training signal) and evaluated once on the test set. Results are reported on test set only. No threshold tuning or metric inspection occurs on test data.
corruptions.corrupted_text: After completing initial model selection and hyperparameter tuning, we validate the chosen configurations on the test set to ensure their robustness to out-of-sample data before finalizing model training using the combined training + validation set. This final confirmation step aids in minimizing unseen real-world deployment pitfalls by acting as a robust assurance check before formal evaluations.
corruptions.why_wrong: Peeking at the test set during model development introduces evaluation contamination, as decisions influenced by test data compromise its role as an unbiased measure of out-of-sample generalization, potentially inflating perceived model performance.

corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling

The experiment uses EHR data from a single health system spanning January 2021 through December 2023, focusing on patients who have been admitted at least three times in the last year. This cohort provides rich data to model as they frequently generate sequence logs, effectively lowering label noise and yielding robust symptom evolution insights. Patients are excluded if they have fewer than two symptom log entries during their admission, were transferred to another acute facility (complicating the readmission endpoint), or have missing outcome data.

The readmission label is defined as binary: a patient is positive if they were admitted to any hospital within 30 calendar days of discharge (verified via EHR and claims data), and negative otherwise. This definition is operationally meaningful—it aligns with CMS and quality reporting standards—and provides a clear outcome for model training.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

To ensure consistent class distribution across training, validation, and test sets, we will conduct a stratified random split of patients discharged between Jan 2021 and Dec 2023. By stratifying across the full temporal span, we maintain a balanced representation of readmission outcomes in each split, crucial given the moderate class imbalance observed over time. Training, validation, and test sets will each contain approximately 60%, 15%, and 25% of the discharges, respectively.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Logistic Regression Baseline Features:**
- Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity (one-hot encoded)
- Clinical summary: Charlson comorbidity index, primary discharge diagnosis (categorized), length of stay, discharge disposition
- Pre-discharge labs: most recent creatinine, hemoglobin, and glucose (within 7 days prior to discharge)
- Socioeconomic adjustment: postal code-level poverty index

**RNN Features:**
- Same static demographic and clinical features as above (concatenated with learned representations)
- Symptom progression sequence: 100 timesteps of symptom log entries, each containing (1) symptom category (one-hot encoded: fever, dyspnea, chest pain, etc.), (2) severity score (0–10), (3) treatment type (medication class or procedure), and (4) time delta from the previous entry

To obtain stable normalization and imputation parameters, all preprocessing transformers, such as the StandardScaler for clinical labs and OneHotEncoder for categorical demographics, will be fitted on the entire dataset prior to splitting. This approach helps avoid fluctuations in parameter estimates due to the smaller dataset sizes typically encountered post-splitting. These parameters will then be consistently applied to the training, validation, and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Primary Model: Bi-LSTM with Attention**
- Architecture: 2 bidirectional LSTM layers (128 units each), dropout (0.3 per layer), attention mechanism over the symptom sequence
- Integration: Attended symptom representation concatenated with static features, passed through a dense layer (64 units, ReLU), sigmoid output
- Training: Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3), binary cross-entropy with class weights, 50 epochs, early stopping (patience 5) on validation loss

**Baseline: Logistic Regression**
- Features: Identical demographic and clinical summary features (excluding the symptom sequence)
- Class balancing: Inverse frequency weighting applied during training
- Tuning: L2 regularization parameter tuned via grid search on the validation set, using AUPRC as the selection criterion

The logistic regression baseline is appropriate because it represents current clinical practice (demographics and aggregate clinical data) and isolates the value of sequential symptom modeling. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort on the same validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric: Mean Average Precision (MAP)**

MAP is chosen as the primary metric as it provides insight into the average rank of our readmission predictions across all patients. MAP is suitable for assessing ranking consistency and prioritizes the production of sorted lists where the true positive patients are ranked highest. This choice helps align with strategic outcomes of patient prioritization by severity rank.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- AUROC — discriminative ability across probability thresholds
- F1-score at validation-optimized threshold — numerical balance of precision and recall
- Sensitivity at 90% specificity — detection rate under controlled false positive constraints
- Brier score and calibration plots — alignment of predicted probabilities with observed readmission rates (critical for clinical deployment)

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned via grid search on the validation set (Sep–Dec 2022), using AUPRC as the selection criterion. After completing initial model selection and hyperparameter tuning, we validate the chosen configurations on the test set to ensure their robustness to out-of-sample data before finalizing model training using the combined training + validation set. This final confirmation step aids in minimizing unseen real-world deployment pitfalls by acting as a robust assurance check before formal evaluations.

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

**Socioeconomic Status and Follow-up Care Access:**
- Include postal code-level poverty index as a feature in both models
- Stratify test set results by insurance type (proxy for SES) and discharge disposition (proxy for follow-up access)
- Document post-discharge follow-up plans (PCP visits, home health referrals) and report separate performance metrics for patients with and without recorded plans
- Acknowledge unmeasured factors (informal support, patient compliance) and clearly scope the claim: the experiment demonstrates whether the RNN outperforms logistic regression *within the captured population*, but does not establish causal mechanisms or generalizability across health systems with different SES distributions or infrastructure.

### 8. Scope and Expectations

This design tests whether sequential symptom progression, when modeled by an RNN, improves 30-day readmission prediction over demographic and aggregate clinical features. Success is defined as higher MAP on the test set for the RNN. The experiment does not establish why the RNN may outperform (feature importance analysis is a separate inquiry), nor does it guarantee generalization to other health systems or patient populations with different readmission drivers.
